coredata.dc:title: A folic acid-sensitive polyfluorene based “turn-off” fluorescence nanoprobe for folate receptor overexpressed cancer cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Roy, Susmita
Gunukula, Anusha
Ghosh, Balaram
Chakraborty, Chanchal
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have developed an efficient approach for folate receptor (FR) over expressed cancer cell targeting and imaging through non-covalent conjugation between folic acid (FA) and a polyfluorene based amphiphilic, fluorescent polymer nanoprobes (CPF) by fluorescence “turn off” mechanism. The pH dependent supramolecular complexation/decomplexation tendency of CPF nanoprobes with FA provides the effective reversible fluorescence “turn off/turn on” of CPF fluorescence. The complete pH dependent complexation/decomplexation phenomena is characterized by photophysical and morphological characterization. The FESEM image study reveals the alteration of nanosphere structure of CPF to interconnected network after complexation with FA at pH 9 and consequently restoration of nanosphere morphology by decomplexation at pH 2. Again, the CPF nanoprobes exhibit 91% fluorescence quenching even in physiological pH 7.4 to reveal that the nanoprobes can be a suitable candidate for FA targeted turn-off fluorescent nanoprobes for FA pre-treated FR-positive cancer cells in vivo study. The fluorescence of CPF nanoprobes is significantly quenched in FA pre-treated FR-positive B16F10 cancer cell whereas it remains unchanged in FR-non overexpressed normal human corneal cell (HCE cells) even after FA pre-treatment. Furthermore, the present study can provide a dissimilar protocol to use a “turn off” fluorescent nanoprobe for FR-positive cancer cell imaging using free FA and fluorescent polymer nanoparticle rather than the conventional direct FA-fluorescent conjugated polymer complex.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyfluorene
Polymer nanoparticles
Fluorescence quenching
Cancer cell imaging
Folic acid

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of solution for spectroscopic study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence “turn on/ turn off” response of the CPF nanoprobes in FA pre-treated FR overexpressed cancer cells.
Chemical formula of cationic polyfluorene CPF and folic acid.
(a) UV–vis and (b) CD spectroscopy of CPF, FA and their assembly in different pH.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of CPF in a water/THF (9:1) mixture and in presence of 200 μM FA at pH 9. (b) The stepwise quenching of photoluminescence of CPF in presence of different concentrations of FA at pH 9. (c) TCSPC plots of CPF in water/THF (9:1) mixture and CPF-FA at different pHs. (d) The fluorogenic titration profile of CPF with FA at 411 nm derived from (b); Inset: the Stern–Volmer plots for photoluminescence quenching of CPF with lower concentrations of FA at pH 9.

(a) The pH dependent photoluminescence spectra of CPF-FA assembly (with 200 μM of FA). (b) Reversibility study of CPF luminescence in CPF-FA assembly upon successive changing of pH between pH 2 and pH 9.
The (a) FESEM and (b) TEM images of CPF. The FESEM images of CPF-FA at (c) pH 9 and (d) pH 2.
Fluorescence quenching of CPF with the interaction of FA at pH 7.4 in PBS buffer condition.

Effect of diverse potentially coexisting substances on the fluorescence of CPF nanoprobe in (a) pH 9 and (b) pH 7.4. The concentration of CPF and FA are 10 μg mL−1 and 200 μM respectively, while the concentration of other coexisting substances are 1000 μM. The fluorescence intensity was monitored at 411 nm.

Fluorescent images of (A) B16F10 and (B) HCE cells after treatment with CPF in FA pre-treated and untreated cells. The images in (a–b) are the bright field images and (c–d) are fluorescence field of B16F10 cell. Accordingly, the images in (e–f) are the bright field images and (g–h) are fluorescence field of HCE cell. The pre-treatment with FA was done using 200 μL of folic acid solution (5 mg mL−1 in PBS) and incubated for 4 h. Cells were incubated with CPF solution (50 μg mL−1) for another 4 h.; Magnification lens for 40 × . Scale bars 50 μm.
Comparison of various methods for detection of folic acid and corresponding FR-positive cancer call imaging.

head.ce:title: A folic acid-sensitive polyfluorene based “turn-off” fluorescence nanoprobe for folate receptor overexpressed cancer cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Birla Institute of Technology and Science (BITS) Pilani, Hyderabad Campus, Jawahar Nagar, Shameerpet Mandal, Hyderabad, 500078, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Birla Institute of Technology and Science (BITS) Pilani

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Birla Institute of Technology and Science (BITS) Pilani, Hyderabad Campus, Jawahar Nagar, Shameerpet Mandal, Hyderabad, 500078, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Birla Institute of Technology and Science (BITS) Pilani

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have developed an efficient approach for folate receptor (FR) over expressed cancer cell targeting and imaging through non-covalent conjugation between folic acid (FA) and a polyfluorene based amphiphilic, fluorescent polymer nanoprobes (CPF) by fluorescence “turn off” mechanism. The pH dependent supramolecular complexation/decomplexation tendency of CPF nanoprobes with FA provides the effective reversible fluorescence “turn off/turn on” of CPF fluorescence. The complete pH dependent complexation/decomplexation phenomena is characterized by photophysical and morphological characterization. The FESEM image study reveals the alteration of nanosphere structure of CPF to interconnected network after complexation with FA at pH 9 and consequently restoration of nanosphere morphology by decomplexation at pH 2. Again, the CPF nanoprobes exhibit 91% fluorescence quenching even in physiological pH 7.4 to reveal that the nanoprobes can be a suitable candidate for FA targeted turn-off fluorescent nanoprobes for FA pre-treated FR-positive cancer cells in vivo study. The fluorescence of CPF nanoprobes is significantly quenched in FA pre-treated FR-positive B16F10 cancer cell whereas it remains unchanged in FR-non overexpressed normal human corneal cell (HCE cells) even after FA pre-treatment. Furthermore, the present study can provide a dissimilar protocol to use a “turn off” fluorescent nanoprobe for FR-positive cancer cell imaging using free FA and fluorescent polymer nanoparticle rather than the conventional direct FA-fluorescent conjugated polymer complex.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times, folate receptor (FR) based cancer targeting and therapeutic study become very thought-provoking as FRs are overexpressed in many of epithelial-derived human cancer cells and the same are minimally expressed in normal human cells [1–5]. Folic acid (FA) is an endogenously non-generated water soluble vitamin B with very high selectivity to FR [6,7]. FA based conjugated probes are widely used for targeted imaging and therapeutic drug delivery in FR overexpressed cancer cells [1–5,8–11]. Till date, a decent number of fluorescent imaging nanoprobes like, quantum dots [12–15], Au-nanoparticles and nanoclusters [5,16,17], polymers [8,9,18–20], polymer supported rare earth complex [21] etc. have been designed to conjugate with FA for targeted cancer cell imaging. However, most of the aforementioned systems have some diverting effect as the emission signal in these type of systems is always in ‘turn on’ state. This type always-on fluorescence probe can afford high signal/background ratio which leads to possibility of false positive result owing to nonspecific adsorption of the emissive probe on non-selective cell surface. In contrast to this type always-on fluorescence probe, a “turn on–turn off” or “turn off–turn on” fluorescence switch mechanism can effectively overcome these concern as the fluorescence response can quench or enhance only upon selective binding to FR overexpressed cancer target cells [22–25]. However, in spite of the imaging superiority, the “turn on–turn off” based fluorescence nanoprobes are still not well explored. In this regard we are introducing a conjugated polymer based fluorescent nanoparticle in which, the bright fluorescence gets quenched after conjugating with folic acid.

Conjugated polymer nanoparticles with high fluorescence quantum yield and exceptional light-harvesting properties are very interesting because of their widespread utilization in the field of bio-imaging, drug delivery, and bio-sensing etc. [26–29]. Polyfluorene (PF) is the simplest regular stepladder-type conjugated polyphenylene to exhibit a bright blue fluorescence with high photoluminescence quantum yield solution state [30–34]. The precise feature of polyfluorene is the ability to produce an amplified spectroscopic response to analytes with which it can form complex [35]. Owing to the spectroscopic response to analytes and superior bio-compatibility, PF polymers are widely used as fluorogenic sensors for different chemical or bio-analytes and bio-imaging [35–42]. The ease of synthesis of PF polymers allows the pre- or post-polymerized modification of PF to incorporate different functional groups depending upon the applications [37–39]. Cationic PFs are characteristic of amphiphilic molecules, in which the backbone and alkyl chains are hydrophobic moieties, whereas the cationic charged quaternary ammonium groups are hydrophilic ones. The cationic PFs could effectively form the polymer nanoparticles in water due to its amphiphilic nature and used as an effective fluorogenic bio-imaging and bio-conjugating agent [43–45].

Again, folic acid (FA) is one of the crucial B vitamins which acts as a coenzyme substrate in many metabolic reactions of amino acids and nucleotides [5–7]. It is also an important nutrient for health especially for pregnant women as it reduces the risk of neural tube birth defects [5]. A low level of FA in the body may upsurge the risk of many diseases, like, coronary heart disease, Alzheimer’s and cancers [46]. Herein, we have synthesized a cationic polyfluorene (CPF) co-polymer having two alkyl pendent groups at 9-position with quaternary ammonium cations in each monomer to produce fluorogenic conjugated polymer nanoparticle in water. CPF polymer nanoparticles can effectually make the supramolecular complex with FA at pH above 7 to quench (turn off) its blue luminescence which is regained to “turn on” by reversible decomplexation at pH lower 7 in in-vitro system. We have introduced this phenomenon for bio-imaging of FR overexpressed cancer cell in a novel way by firstly treating the overexpressed FRs in targeted cancer cell with FA and then complexing the FR binded FA with luminescent CPF to “turn off” its luminescence inside the cancer cell (Scheme 1 ). The CPF nanoprobe will get attached to the FRFA complex on the cell surface which will be eventually transported inside the cell via a receptor-mediated endocytosis. We have compared the cancer cell imaging phenomena of FR-positive cancer cell with normal human cells (HCE cell) by CPF nanoprobe to get better insight of comparison. With best of our knowledge, this is the first report to use a “turn off” fluorescent probe for cancer cell imaging using free FA and fluorescent polymer nanoparticle with FR overexpressed cancer cell.

All the chemicals used here were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich with highest purity grade and used as supplied. The solvents like tetrahydrofuran, ethanol etc. were supplied by S D Fine-Chem Limited with HPLC Grade. Deionized water (18 MU cm, Millipore Milli-Q water) was used to prepare the solutions where necessary. Cationic amphiphilic polymer CPF was synthesized by polymerizing 2,7-dibromo-9,9′-bis(6″- bromohexyl)fluorene with 1,4-phenyldiboronic acid by the Suzuki cross-coupling polymerization and afterward quarternization using triethylamine (TEA) according to our previous report [31,32,44]. The molecular weight (Mw ) of polymer the precursor poly(9,9′-bis(6″- bromohexyl)fluorene-co-alt-1,4-phenylene), was determined to be ∼8000 Da with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 1.75 by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) using tetrahydrofuran (THF) as the eluent and polystyrene as a standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of solution for spectroscopic study
ce:section.ce:para: The CPF polymer is sparingly soluble in water and to increase the concentration of these polymers, a THF–water mixture was used for spectroscopic studies. 1 mg cationic polyfluorene CPF was dissolved in 100 mL solvent (10% THF in water) at pH 7. Folic acid solution (2 mM) is prepared in aqueous buffer solution at pH 9. The pH of the CPF-FA assembly was adjusted by adding dilute acid or base and measuring the resultant pH by using a bench-top probe pH-meter (IQ Scientific Instruments, Model-IQ240) which was calibrated with buffer of pH = 7.00 and pH = 4.00 prior to each measurement.

The UV–vis spectra of the compounds were studied using a JASCO/V-650 (190–900 nm) UV–vis spectrophotometer using a quartz cuvette with 1 cm path length. Fluorescence studies were done in a JASCO/FP-6300 (190–900 nm) fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 362 nm. The circular dichroism (CD) study of the solutions were performed in a spectrophotometer (JASCO, model J-1500) in a 1 cm quartz cuvette.

For evaluation of the morphology of CPF and CPF-FA assembly at different pH, field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) was performed by drop casting the compounds on glass cover slip followed by drying in air at room temperature. The sample coated glass cover slips were finally dried in oven at 60 °C. It was coated with gold for 30 s prior to observation through a FESEM instrument by FEI, Apreo S with a 20 kV operating voltage.

Cell imaging studies were carried out using B16 F10 cells (cancer cell lines with over expression of folate receptors) as well as Human Corneal Epithelial (HCE) cells (normal cell lines). Briefly, B16 F10 cells were seeded in 12 well plate at a density of 50,000 cells/well in 500 μL of DMEM medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After 24 h, 6 out of 12 wells were saturated with folic acid solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) and the other 6 wells were kept as control (without folic acid treatment), and the cells were incubated for 4 h. Then, the media was replaced with freshly prepared CPF solution (1000 μL; 50 μg/mL in DMEM media) from all the wells and incubated for 4 h. The treatments were carried out in duplicate. Later the polymer solution was pipetted out and the wells were thoroughly rinsed with PBS to wash out the dead cells as well as left out polymer solution if any. The fluorescence of CPF inside the cells was observed under the fluorescence microscope (Leica Microsystems LAS version 4.8.0 (Switzerland) Ltd.) at 40x and the images were captured. The same procedure was repeated using Human Corneal Epithelial (HCE) cells, in DMEM-F12 medium.

The luminescent cationic polyfluorene polymer CPF shown in Fig. 1 was synthesized by polymerizing 2,7-dibromo-9,9′-bis(6″- bromohexyl)fluorene with 1,4-phenyldiboronic acid by Pd-catalyzed Suzuki cross-coupling polymerization reaction followed by quarternization using triethylamine (TEA) according to our previous report [31,32,44]. The molecular weight derived from GPC study and PDI of CPF polymer were ∼8000 Da and 1.75 respectively. The polymer is sparsely soluble in water while very nicely soluble in tetrahydrofuran, DMSO, DMF etc. We have prepared a CPF solution by dissolving 1 mg cationic polyfluorene in 100 mL solvent (10% THF in water) at pH 7. Another stock solution of folic acid solution (2 mM) is prepared in aqueous buffer solution at pH 9. We have prepared two CPF-FA assembly by adding 200 μM (final concentration in the assembly) of FA into CPF solution at two different pH; one is alkaline pH at 9 and another acidic pH at 2.

The UV–vis spectroscopic study was performed to verify the binding of FA with the cationic polymer CPF. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2 a) of the cationic CPF exhibit a single absorption at 362 nm due to π-π* transition of fluorene core [31,44]. FA displays its characteristic absorption at 278 nm along with a small hump having maxima at 352 nm [47]. After addition of 200 μM FA into the CPF solution at pH 9, the overall assembly shows characteristic CPF band at 370 nm and folic acid band at 304 nm. This red shifting of CPF band of 8 nm and FA band of 26 nm is due to the supramolecular assembly formation by electrostatic interaction between cationic CPF and anionic folic acid at pH 9. At alkaline pH (pH 9) the FA remains as folate anion and the –COO- groups provides a strong ionic interaction with positively charged quaternary ammonium cations in CPF polymers so that the coiled CPF polymer chains get some shorts of uncoiling which results in the red shifting in UV–vis spectroscopy. Again, when we adjust the pH of the assembly to an acidic pH (pH 2), the corresponding characteristic bands of CPF and FA get shifted to their original position. The acidic condition of the system can generate the folate anion back to its original FA state and the ionic interactions between the –COO- and quaternary ammonium cations are distorted to individual component back to its original state by effectively breaking the assembly.

The CD spectra of CPF, FA and their complexes in different pH was executed in solution to gain more insight into the assembly through ionic interaction between chromophoric polymer and chiral FA. The CD spectra shown in Fig. 2b reveals the almost straight line in case of CPF surmising the random orientation of completely achiral polymer. The Spectra of FA exhibits a positive peak at 280 nm along with a negative peak above 350 nm which are fairly consistent with the absorption peak in UV–vis spectroscopy at 278 nm and hump above 350 nm. The appearance of the CD signals in FA is owing to its chirality. Again, the CPF-FA complex at pH 9 shows a strong bisignated cotton effect at the position of the absorption band of FA with zero crossing close to the absorption maximum (278 nm) is clearly observed. Interestingly, a significant peak with positive cotton effect is generated at the π–π* transition absorption band of CPF in the complex. The generation the positive peak at 368 nm is exclusively due to the formation of CPF-FA complex at pH 9 through ionic interaction with chiral FA and achiral polymer and operative induction of chirality in the CPF-FA complex [48]. However, at pH 2, the CD spectra of the CPF-FA assembly is showing only the positive peak for chiral FA and the absence of peak regarding CPF absorption. So, at pH 2, we can assume the breaking up the CPF-FA assembly as stated earlier and the individual components are resulting. As, CPF is achiral and the induced chirality is lost due to the breaking up the complex at pH 2, the CPF is not showing any trace of induced chirality around its absorption.

In photoluminescence spectroscopy in Fig. 3 a, cationic CPF solution shows bright blue emission at 411 nm which is the characteristic emission of polyfluorene containing polymers [31,32,44]. After addition of 200 μM of FA to CPF solution at pH 9, the bright blue luminescence of CPF quenches (Fig. 3a). To get better insight, the photoluminescence titration was performed in that pH by successive addition of FA. As shown in Fig. 3b, fluorescence intensity of CPF at 411 nm gradually decreases with the continuous addition of FA concentration, which indicates FA could significantly quench the luminescence of fluorescent polymer CPF by effective CPF-FA complex formation through ionic interaction. Form this plot it is concluded that the florescence quenching of CPF by FA starts at very low concentration and saturates above 160 μM. Interestingly, during fluorescence quenching titration by the stepwise addition of FA, the fluorescence peaks are unaltered its peak position inferring the static quenching is predominant here rather than a dynamic quenching [37–39,49].

To evaluate the insight about the nature of the quenching mechanism, we have studied the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurement with CPF solution and compared it with CPF-FA complex with 200 μM of FA at pH 9. From the Fig. 3c, it is envisioned that the average lifetimes of CPF and CPF-FA complex at pH 9 do not change upon addition of a quencher FA. From the corresponding time constants, the average lifetimes are calculated to be 0.514 ns and 0.540 ns respectively. As there is no change in the average lifetime even after addition of FA to CPF, we can conclude that static quenching is prominent after addition of bio-analytes FA [39,49].

The photoluminescence intensities with respect to FA concentrations are plotted in Fig. 3d. Generally, in static quenching, the quencher (here FA) forms a ground-state complex with the fluorophore and consequently quenches after excitation. Herein, the CPF is forming a complex with FA by effective electrostatic interaction at pH 9. The quenching constant Ksv in static quenching equals the apparent complex formation constant between the quencher and the fluorophore and can be determined using the Stern–Volmer equation [4,37–39,50,51],
I
0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
Q

Here, I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore, I is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of a quencher (FA), Ksv is the static quenching constant, and [Q] is the quencher concentration. The Stern–Volmer equation divulges that I0/I increases in direct linearly with the concentration of the quencher. We have plotted the I0/I vs. concentration of [FA] in the inset of Fig. 3d and determined the Ksv from the slope of the plot. The value of Ksv is calculated to be 2.2 × 104 M−1 which implies very good complexation between the CPF and FA at pH 9. Again, from the linearity of I0/I vs. concentration of [FA] in lower concentration range (R2 = 0.997), we have evaluated the limit of detection (LoD) of FA was to be 31 nM (3 S/N).

As the key factor of the assembly between CPF and FA is the electrostatic interaction between quaternary ammonium cation and negative folate anions, the interaction would be reliant on the pH of the system. The effect of pH was elucidated by the pH dependent photoluminescence study by taking initial CPF-FA complex at pH 9. The Fig. 4 a reveals the quenched fluorescence of CPF in CPF-FA complex is gradually recovered with the lowering of pH. At very high acidic pH (at pH 2) it restores back its 50% photoluminescence intensity. At acidic pH, the -COO- of FA can procedures back -COOH and the interaction with CPF is getting lower. The reversibility study of this pH dependent luminescence “turn off/turn on” in respective pHs is also carried out by alternate pH changing in alkaline pH (pH 9) and acidic pH (pH 2) of the CPF-FA solution system and the respective luminescence changes are shown in Fig. 4b. We have performed up to the 5th cycle and it exhibits very promising repeatability of the luminescence and the trend of reversibility clearly provides the idea that beyond fifth cycle the system should be continuing its reversibility.

The pH dependent assembly and disassembly between CPF and FA will also affect the corresponding morphology of the polymer CPF. To get better insight about the morphological change during the assembled/disassembled process, we studied the FESEM image study of CPF and CPF-FA assembly in pH 2 and pH 9. The FESEM image of CPF in Fig. 5 a reveals the self-assembled nanosphere morphology for the polymer in water/THF solvent mixture (9:1). The self-assembly of the amphiphilic polymeric system having hydrophilic ionic alkyl group and hydrophobic polyfluorene main chain effectively provides the nanosphere of ˜100−400 nm diameter. The TEM image in Fig. 5b divulges that the aggregated nanospheres are solid. In a self-assembled polymer structure, the aromatic fluorene moieties in main chain provides a higher degree of π-π stacking which results in the solid nanosphere formation [44,45,52]. Again after complex formation with FA at pH 9, nanosphere morphology of the CPF is completely destroyed and an interconnected assembled nanostructured network is prominent in Fig. 5c. However, when the pH decreases to 2, it reinstates the nanosphere morphology of CPF shown in Fig. 5d. The complexation of CPF and FA at pH 9 generates the interconnected network structure which is decomplexed at acidic pH 2 to produce the CPF back in its original nanosphere shape [38]. So, the morphological study directly proves the reversible nature of the complexation/decomplexation between CPF-FA depending on the pH values.

The photophysical and morphological experiments discussed above envisioned the pH dependent electrostatic interaction between the CPF and FA. Taking the electrostatic interaction based luminescence “turn off” into account, we can deploy the CPF polymer nanosphere for cancer cell imaging by interacting with the FA in a folate receptor overexpressed cancer cell pre-treated with the FA. However, before going to imaging experiment, we have studied again the photoluminescence quenching of CPF with FA in PBS buffer of pH 7.4 which is biological pH condition. The Fig. 6 depicts the luminescence of CPF is quenched by ˜91% in presence of FA at pH 7.4. So, the fluorescence probe CPF would be highly applicable for cancer cell imaging in biological condition using fluorescence “turn off” mechanism.

Not only the sensitivity, but selectivity is another major factor for a sensor to be used in bio-imaging. To investigate the selectivity of the CPF nanoprobe towards FA, we have performed the fluorescence response of the probe toward various potentially coexisting substances, such as Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, oxalic acid (OA), ascorbic acid (AA), and citric acid (CA), urea, serine (Ser), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Iso-leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Meth), glycine (Gly), Arginine (Arg), tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyro) and valine (Val) (1000 μM). As shown in Fig. 7 a-b, the intrinsic fluorescence of CPF nanoprobe at 411 nm exhibits no significant change by the introduction of coexisting substances with a concentration of 1000 μM in pH 9 and 7.4. The CPF nanoprobe provides the fluorescence quenching selectively with FA even after addition of a low concentration of 200 μM. The above results indicate that the CPF nanoprobe is highly selective for FA over other common biologically related substances and provides the selective requirements for bio-imaging applications.

The quenching of CPF in folate receptor over expressed cancer cell lines (B16 F10 cells) as well as normal cell lines (HCE cells) following treatment with polymer solution in FA treated as well as FA untreated (control) cells was assessed by visualizing the cells under fluorescence microscope. Generally, the FR are overexpressed on the cell membrane and that FR can capture the pre-treated FA from the extracellular environment. The CPF nanoprobe in the extracellular environment can attach with the FR bound FA due to high selectivity of CPF nanoprobe with FA. After attaching of CPF over FA bound FR, it can be transported inside the cell via a receptor-mediated endocytosis and the further effective complexation in the intracellular condition can provide the effective CPF fluorescence quenching. Fluorescence images of B16F10 and HCE cells after CPF nanoprobe treatment are shown in the Fig. 8 . Fluorescence intensity of CPF in B16F10 cells is found to be significantly decreased after pre-treatment with FA (Fig. 8c and d), whereas, the decrement in the intensity with HCE cells was negligible (Fig. 8g and h). Nonetheless, this can be because FA has more affinity to the folate receptors of B16F10 cells compared to normal cell lines (HCE). Later, the addition of CPF solution to the FA saturated B16F10 cells, results in the quenching of the polymer fluorescence, which can be observed from the fluorescent images. The quenching was high in pre-treated B16F10 cells in comparison with the one without any pre-treatment. In HCE cells, the density of FA would be quite minimum even after pre-treatment with FA as it doesn’t have many FR. Thus, after treatment with CPF, the fluorescence of CPF would not be changed much in HCE cell due to absence of FA. Hence, quenching of CPF in FA saturated B16F10 cells is considerable stronger with reference to the FA untreated B16F10 cells as well as HCE, showing that the fluorescence of CPF is effectively quenched in the presence of pre-treated FA. We have inserted a comparison table to compare the features of developed method in this work with other previously reported similar methods at Table 1 .

We have used the free FA and CPF separately for the FR-positive cancer cell imaging considering an added advantage. The microenvironment of the extracellular matrix of tumors in vivo is acidic (pH 6.2–6.9) while the intracellular pH is little alkaline (˜7.4) [55]. The assembly between CPF and FA showed pH dependent luminescence "turn off/turn on". There would be a chance to disassemble the CPF-FA conjugate in extracellular acidic pH condition if the CPF-FA conjugate is delivered. As, we have delivered the free CPF nanoprobe which would be stable in the extracellular little acidic pH condition as it does not have any acid sensitive group, and would be attached with the FR-bound FA on cell membrane. The FA attached CPF will undergo inside the cell through receptor mediated endocytosis process and strengthen the effective complexation in intracellular alkaline pH condition to quench the CPF fluorescence.

In summary, we have developed a cationic polyfluorene based amphiphilic, fluorescent polymer nanosphere probe for cancer cell targeting and imaging by fluorescence “turn on-turn off” mechanism. The CPF polymer nanoprobes have significant pH dependent supramolecular complexation/decomplexation tendency with FA to provide the effective reversible fluorescence “turn off/turn on” of CPF fluorescence. The reversible pH dependent complexation/decomplexation phenomena is envisioned by morphological study with FESEM which reveals the alteration of nanosphere morphology of CPF to interconnected network after complexation with FA at alkaline pH range and further restoration of nanosphere morphology by decomplexation at acidic pH. Furthermore, the CPF nanoprobes provides effective fluorescence quenching with FA even in physiological pH. The CPF nanoprobes can effectively distinguish the cells with different expression levels of FR. The fluorescence of the nanoprobes is considerably quenched in FA pre-treated B16F10 cancer cell where the overexpressed FRs are bound with FA. This applicability has been confirmed by comparing the phenomena with normal HCE cells where no alteration of fluorescence is seen even after FA pre-treatment. Moreover, this study can provide a different protocol to use a “turn off” fluorescent probe for cancer cell imaging using free FA and fluorescent polymer nanoparticle with FR overexpressed cancer cell rather than the conventional direct FA-fluorescent conjugated polymer complex.
The authors report no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank DST, Govt. of India for DST INSPIRE Faculty award (Application Reference Number: DST/INSPIRE/04/2016/002255) to execute the research. Authors also thank Prof. Sudip Malik, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata for his immense support regarding this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Discovery and development of folic-acid-Based receptor targeting for imaging and therapy of cancer and inflammatory diseases
Tumor detection using folate receptor-targeted imaging agents
Fluorescent chemical probes for accurate tumor diagnosis and targeting therapy
Non-covalent decoration of carbon dots with folic acid via a polymer-assisted strategy for fast and targeted cancer cell fluorescence imaging
Fabrication of folic acid-sensitive gold nanoclusters for turn-on fluorescent imaging of overexpression of folate receptor in tumor cells

Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
Folate and colorectal cancer: an evidence‐based critical review
Folic acid-conjugated fluorescent polymer for up-regulation folate receptor expression study via targeted imaging of tumor cells
Folic acid conjugated amino acid-based star polymers for active targeting of Cancer cells
A pH-sensitive doxorubicin prodrug based on folate-conjugated BSA for tumor-targeted drug delivery
Distinguishing folate-receptor-positive cells from folate-receptor-negative cells using a fluorescence off−on nanoprobe
Quantum dot–folic acid conjugates as potential photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy of cancer
Folic acid-CdTe quantum dot conjugates and their applications for cancer cell targeting
Folic acid encapsulated graphene quantum dots for ratiometric pH sensing and specific multicolor imaging in living cells
Synthesis of luminescent carbon dots with ultrahigh quantum yield and inherent folate receptor positive cancer cell targetability
Folate-modified gold nanoclusters as near-infrared fluorescent probes for tumor imaging and therapy
Near infrared fluorescent trypsin stabilized gold nanoclusters as surface plasmon enhanced energy transfer biosensor and in vivo cancer Imaging Bioprobe
